title: Erling Haaland Is Everywhere at the World Cup. Most of It Is AI
gist: AI-generated deepfakes of athletes like Erling Haaland are being embraced as fan art rather than feared, shifting the celebrity economy from access-dependent (the star must do real things) to audience-driven (fans synthesize on-demand content that fits the character). The old fantasy machine needed raw material from the star; the new one needs only the audience's willingness to keep the story going.

insights.claim: Deepfake panic is giving way to opt-in deepfake consumption: even after fact-checkers corrected a viral fake Haaland video, audiences knowingly kept sharing it because it fit the character, signaling that provenance matters less than fit to fans.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Content-authenticity and detection tooling may have a smaller addressable market in entertainment than assumed; watch demand shift toward generation/creation tools over detection.
insights.quote: Though much of the public was, in fact, fooled by the AI video, a meaningful share of the audience is actively opting in and sharing nonetheless.

insights.claim: A single fake Haaland video (traced to a June TikTok skit by Chinese comedian Jin Long) racked up more than 31 million views on one X post within days despite being debunked, demonstrating that corrections do not stop AI content velocity.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Virality economics reward AI-fabricated celebrity content regardless of truth; platforms monetizing engagement have weak incentives to police it.
insights.quote: One post on X sharing the video racked up more than 31 million views in mere days. But here's the thing: It isn't him.
insights.locator: Last week, somewhere amid the World Cup frenzy

insights.claim: The tolerance for AI fakes tracks affection: audiences suspend disbelief for content about people or brands they already like, as when the 2023 Balenciaga Pope image drove more praise for the coat than concern over AI.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Brands with strong affinity can exploit AI virality with low backlash risk; likability is the moat that converts deepfakes into marketing.
insights.quote: if you like someone or something enough, you'll suspend your disbelief and just roll with it
insights.locator: That same year, the Balenciaga Pope

insights.claim: Stardom is shifting from tight control of one's own image to becoming an 'open-source character' vivid and meme-able enough that AI and fans do the promotion, loosely tethering the persona from the actual human.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Talent/IP monetization models built on controlling likeness may erode; value migrates to whoever owns the character and its distribution rather than image lockdown.
insights.quote: The celebrity, therefore, becomes something like an open-source character, only loosely tethered to the human who has the face.
insights.locator: If the old model of stardom

insights.claim: The celebrity economy is restructuring from access-dependent to audience-dependent: the old model monetized proximity to the star while the new one runs purely on the audience's willingness to keep generating and sharing the story via AI.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Disintermediation risk for agencies, broadcasters, and rights-holders whose value was gating access to the star; upside for AI generation platforms and fan-content ecosystems.
insights.quote: The old celebrity economy depended on access to the star. The new one depends only on the audience's willingness to keep the story going.
insights.locator: But the old fantasy machine needed raw material

insights.claim: Fan-generated 'fanon' (audience-invented material filling gaps the canon leaves) is now AI-synthesizable on demand, meaning fans no longer need the athlete to produce lore and the character absorbs the fabricated content seamlessly.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Content supply for fandoms becomes near-infinite and marginal-cost-zero; scarcity of official content loses pricing power.
insights.quote: You no longer need the athlete to generate the lore; the audience can synthesize it on demand, and the character absorbs it seamlessly.
insights.locator: So once a footballer becomes a character

insights.claim: A WSC Sports report found Gen Z feels more connected to individual athletes than to teams, inverting the traditional team-centric loyalty model that underpins league and franchise value.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Athlete-level IP and personal brands may capture value historically held by teams/leagues; watch player-owned media and endorsement economics vs franchise valuations.
insights.quote: Gen Z in particular feels more connected to individual athletes than they do teams
insights.locator: They're also now getting the full fandom treatment

insights.claim: An Oliver Wyman survey found social media content from athletes is the single largest driver of Gen Z sports engagement, outweighing traditional broadcast and highlight consumption.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Ad and sponsorship dollars should follow athlete social channels over broadcast; media-rights valuations face structural pressure from creator-style distribution.
insights.quote: social media content from athletes is the single largest driver of Gen Z sports engagement
insights.locator: They're also now getting the full fandom treatment

insights.claim: Athletes are increasingly consumed as evolving fictional-style characters with lore, canon, and character arcs rather than through highlights or post-match interviews.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Content formats and platforms optimized for serialized character narratives (short-form, meme, edits) capture attention over traditional sports media.
insights.quote: Athletes are no longer consumed solely through highlights or post-match interviews but as evolving characters with recognizable quirks and storylines.
insights.locator: This is increasingly how sports fandom works online

insights.claim: China has become a primary manufacturing hub for athlete memes: Haaland fronted a Chinese herbal-drink commercial, attempted Mandarin, was rechristened 'Habao,' and the viral deepfake originated from this Chinese cottage industry of AI Haaland edits.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Western athlete brand value is increasingly minted in Chinese platforms; sponsorship and localization strategies targeting Douyin/Weibo can drive global virality.
insights.quote: the striker has already become somewhat of a meme sensation
insights.locator: And the Haaland fake didn't spawn from nowhere

insights.claim: Haaland launched official Douyin and Weibo accounts as his China popularity exploded and quickly amassed millions of followers, showing athletes moving directly onto Chinese platforms to capture organically generated fandom.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Direct-to-platform athlete presence in China is a monetizable channel; watch for endorsement and merchandising flows following account launches.
insights.locator: As his popularity in China exploded

insights.claim: Fan embrace of AI likenesses has multi-year precedent: the @deeptomcruise deepfakes drew millions of delighted views in 2021, and a 2023 AI track mimicking Drake and The Weeknd was streamed enthusiastically before labels could pull it.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Rights-holders repeatedly lose the takedown race against AI fan content; enforcement-based IP defense is structurally reactive and slow.
insights.quote: an AI-generated track mimicking Drake and The Weeknd that appeared in 2023 created its own fan hype, getting streamed enthusiastically before the labels could get it pulled

insights.claim: Haaland's unpolished, contradiction-driven persona (a 6-foot-5 on-pitch 'goal machine' who posts nostril-angle selfies and bald filters to 3.3 million Snapchat followers) is precisely what makes him meme-able, contrasting with brand-protected, media-trained modern footballers.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: Authenticity and self-parody, not brand control, drive fandom scale; the meme-ability of a persona is a leading indicator of commercial reach.
insights.quote: His personal Snapchat account of 3.3 million followers and climbing is a masterclass in unpolished celebrity.
insights.locator: After all, Haaland was always going to be a star

insights.claim: Kylian Mbappe demonstrates the same pattern: 'Dictator Mbappe' AI memes recasting him as Mao or Kim Jong Un, rooted in a 2023 kebab dispute, resurged tenfold during the tournament and now appear in fully AI-rendered fiction.
insights.speaker: Megan Tomos
insights.so_what: The character-ification and AI-ification effect is not athlete-specific but a repeatable dynamic across star footballers, implying scalable content ecosystems.
insights.quote: the "Dictator Mbappé" AI memes recast him as Mao and Kim Jong Un, usually soundtracked—with the internet's flair for tonal chaos—by an ominous nasheed
insights.locator: French player Kylian Mbappé is another footballer
